stories.headline: Update: Missing Oscar Found in Frankfurt After TSA Forced Anti-Putin Documentary Filmmaker to Check It
stories.summary:
Lufthansa confirmed Saturday that an Academy Award statue belonging to Pavel Talankin, co-director of the Best Feature Documentary winner "Mr Nobody Against Putin," has been located in Frankfurt after going missing during a checked-bag incident at JFK Airport. Talankin had carried the Oscar in his carry-on bag for a flight to Germany, but TSA stopped him at JFK, saying the statuette could be used as a weapon and requiring him to check it. When he landed in Frankfurt, the Oscar was gone.

Lufthansa told the BBC: "The Oscar statue has now been located and is safely in our care in Frankfurt," and said the airline was in direct contact with Talankin to arrange its return "as quickly as possible." The airline added that an internal review of the circumstances was ongoing and expressed regret for the inconvenience.

The documentary's executive producer Robin Hessman said Talankin had flown multiple times previously with both his Oscar and his BAFTA without incident, and disputed the necessity of forcing him to check the award. "This wouldn't have happened to Leonardo DiCaprio," she said. The BBC has contacted the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences and the TSA for comment.

"Mr Nobody Against Putin" documents the intensification of war propaganda in a Russian school following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Talankin is now exiled from Russia for his safety; Russia has banned the documentary from streaming platforms on grounds that it "propagates extremism and terrorism."

stories.headline: Update: UK Terror Threat Raised to Severe as PM Starmer Calls for Tougher Action on Antisemitic Protest Chants
stories.summary:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer called Saturday for tougher law enforcement action against certain chants at pro-Palestinian protests, saying some marches may need to be banned, as concerns mounted over the safety of British Jews following the stabbing of two Jewish men in London's Golders Green neighborhood. A 45-year-old man was charged Friday with attempted murder in connection with the Wednesday attack, which police classified as an act of terrorism.

The UK's terror threat level was raised from "substantial" to "severe" — the second-highest on a five-point scale — meaning intelligence agencies consider an attack highly likely within six months. Authorities said the change reflected not only the Golders Green attack but also a broader increase in danger from Islamist and extreme right-wing threats. Metropolitan Police Commissioner Mark Rowley warned that British Jews face "their greatest ever threat" and blamed social media for mainstreaming antisemitism.

Starmer singled out the chant "globalize the intifada" as warranting enforcement action, while affirming that the right to protest must be protected. He suggested repeated pro-Palestinian marches have had a "cumulative effect" contributing to a rise in antisemitic incidents. The Community Security Trust recorded 3,700 antisemitic incidents across the UK in 2025, up from 1,662 in 2022.

The development follows a string of recent incidents including arson attacks on synagogues and Jewish sites in London. Rowley described Jews as being "on everybody's list" of extremist targets, citing threats from far-right, far-left, Islamist, and state-linked Iranian-related groups. Community members told CBS News they now live in constant fear of attack.

stories.headline: Update: Aung San Suu Kyi Moved to House Arrest After Five Years in Military Detention, Myanmar Junta Says
stories.summary:
Myanmar's military junta announced Saturday that former leader Aung San Suu Kyi, 80, has been transferred to house arrest after more than five years in military detention following the February 2021 coup. State media published a statement from coup leader Min Aung Hlaing saying her remaining sentence had been "commuted to be served at the designated residence," though the image released alongside the announcement was taken in 2022.

Suu Kyi's son, Kim Aris, expressed deep skepticism, saying he had no proof his mother was even alive and that the photograph was "meaningless" given its age. Her legal team told Reuters they had received no direct notification of the transfer. Aris said he would not believe the announcement until he was allowed direct communication or an independent party could verify her condition and whereabouts.

Analysts suggested the move is part of a broader public relations effort by the military junta to reduce international isolation. Coup leader Min Aung Hlaing held elections earlier this year restoring a nominally democratic government while keeping military leaders in power, and has achieved recent battlefield gains against armed opposition groups. Former adviser Sean Turnell told the BBC the junta is "trying to convince the world that it's a legitimate government."

Suu Kyi came to power in 2015 following democratic reforms and spent decades as a pro-democracy activist before the coup. She had previously spent more than 15 years under house arrest during earlier periods of military rule. Her 33-year sentence on charges widely dismissed as fabricated has been reduced several times. Her lawyers have not seen her in more than three years; her family has had no contact for over two years.

stories.headline: Rats and Disease Infest Gaza Displacement Camps, Affecting 1.45 Million People as Humanitarian Crisis Deepens
stories.summary:
A severe rodent and pest infestation has taken hold across displacement camps in Gaza, affecting an estimated 1.45 million people, according to a survey cited by UN agencies. Aid workers are calling the situation a public health crisis, with rats, urban weasels and other pests biting children, the sick and elderly, and contaminating food and water supplies. Raw sewage running through overcrowded campsites and accumulating piles of rubbish have created ideal breeding conditions for rodents.

The BBC documented multiple cases of injury, including a four-year-old girl bitten on the hand by a weasel while sleeping, who subsequently suffered days of fever and vomiting after receiving a tetanus injection. A diabetic grandmother described having parts of her toes bitten off due to nerve damage that prevented her from feeling the attacks. Social media footage has circulated widely showing rodents attacking newborns, the sick, and elderly.

The World Health Organization's local representative Dr. Reinhilde Van De Weert called the infestations "the predictable consequence of a collapsed living environment." More than six months after the U.S. brokered a Gaza ceasefire deal, no reconstruction has taken place and Palestinian officials say 200,000 caravans needed as temporary housing have not been delivered. Israel's Cogat body, which controls Gaza's crossings, said it was working with international organizations to address sanitation needs.

Regular deadly airstrikes continue, with Israel saying it is targeting Hamas. Hamas, which triggered the conflict with its October 2023 assault on Israel and mass hostage-taking, has not committed to disarming. Displaced families report keeping vigil through the night to protect children from animal attacks, with many saying sleep has become impossible.

stories.headline: Update: Suspect in Trump Press Dinner Shooting — Key Details Remain Disputed
stories.summary:
Nearly a week after Cole Tomas Allen, 31, allegedly attempted to attack President Trump at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner at the Washington Hilton, investigators and prosecutors are still unable to confirm whether Allen actually shot a Secret Service officer. Trump and senior officials initially stated that an officer was shot and survived due to a bulletproof vest, but court documents filed by government attorneys do not explicitly allege the suspect fired the shot that struck the agent.

Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche walked back his own Sunday statement on the matter by Monday, saying ballistics were still being analyzed. Court filings describe Allen charging through a security magnetometer with a long gun and a Secret Service officer being shot once in the chest, but leave open the question of who fired the round. Allen was found armed with a semi-automatic handgun, a pump-action shotgun, and three knives.

Legal analysts warn that contradictory public statements by law enforcement could give defense attorneys grounds to challenge the government's case. The Justice Department, Secret Service, and U.S. Attorney's office have declined to provide further comment. The discrepancy between the president's public assertions and the formal legal record has drawn scrutiny as the investigation continues.

stories.headline: Trump Announces 25% Tariffs on EU Cars and Trucks, Escalating Trade Tensions
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump announced Friday that he will raise tariffs on cars and trucks imported from the European Union to 25%, escalating an already strained transatlantic trade relationship. Trump claimed the EU was not adhering to a previously negotiated trade deal struck at his Turnberry golf course in Scotland, though he declined to specify how the EU had failed to comply when pressed by reporters.

The European Commission pushed back, stating the EU was implementing the agreement in line with standard legislative practice and had kept U.S. officials fully informed. The Commission said it would keep "all options open" to protect EU interests if the U.S. took measures inconsistent with the joint statement. The automotive sector is a particularly sensitive target — car manufacturing represents a substantial share of several major European economies, including Germany and France.

The tariff hike comes after months of stalled negotiations over steel and aluminium and follows European Parliament approval of the broader trade deal in March, which included a clause allowing suspension if the U.S. was deemed to have engaged in economic coercion or threatened member states' territorial integrity. Trump urged European automakers to shift production to the United States, promising no tariff for cars built on American soil. The announcement rattled global markets and signals a potential breakdown of the Turnberry framework that had offered EU exporters temporary relief from steeper "Liberation Day" levies.

stories.headline: Fifth Circuit Restricts Mail-Order Access to Abortion Pill Mifepristone
stories.summary:
The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals issued an order Friday temporarily reinstating the requirement that mifepristone — the most commonly used abortion pill in the United States — be obtained in person rather than through mail-order pharmacy or telemedicine. The ruling overrides a lower court decision that had paused the case while the Trump administration conducts an FDA review, and stems from a lawsuit brought by the state of Louisiana.

The decision effectively suspends a 2023 FDA regulation that had permanently lifted the in-person dispensing requirement first loosened during the COVID-19 pandemic. The appeals court ruled that the FDA's action undermined Louisiana's abortion ban, writing that "every abortion facilitated by FDA's action cancels Louisiana's ban on medical abortions." The order will remain in effect as the underlying case is litigated.

Supporters of the ruling called it a significant victory. Louisiana Attorney General Liz Murrill said the Biden-era rule "facilitated the deaths of thousands of Louisiana babies," and Marjorie Dannenfelser of Susan B Anthony Pro-Life America praised it as a win for "victims and survivors of Biden's reckless mail-order abortion drug regime." Critics were sharply opposed: ACLU attorney Julia Kaye called the decision one that "defies clear science and settled law," and New York Attorney General Letitia James said abortion access — including medication abortion — remains fully legal in her state and that her office will continue to protect it. In 2024, the Supreme Court had unanimously rejected a separate effort to restrict mifepristone access, but left the door open to further legal challenges.

stories.headline: Turkey Arrests More Than 500 at May Day Rallies in Istanbul
stories.summary:
Turkish police arrested more than 500 people and deployed tear gas and water cannons against demonstrators attempting to march to Taksim Square in Istanbul on International Labour Day. The square has been sealed off to protests since 2013, and police blocked access overnight ahead of Friday's demonstrations. The CHD lawyers' group, which was present at the rallies, confirmed the arrest toll.

Among those confronted by police was Erkan Bas, president of the Turkish Workers' Party, who was part of a group in the Mecidiyekoy district that breached a police barricade before being dispersed with water cannons. Permitted rallies went ahead in other districts and cities including Kadikoy, Kartal, Izmir, Diyarbakir, and Ankara, where groups gathered at approved locations under heavy security.

President Recep Tayyip Erdogan posted a Labor Day greeting on social media, saying he congratulated workers "who earn their living through their labor." The annual crackdown on Taksim Square marches has become a flashpoint in Turkey's ongoing tensions between the government and labor and opposition movements, particularly as the country continues to face political pressure following a series of high-profile arrests of opposition figures earlier this year.
